title: Stair riser and tread limits
text: Residential stairs have a maximum riser height and a minimum tread depth, and the risers and treads within a single flight must be consistent. The greatest riser height must not exceed the smallest by more than 3/8 inch across the flight, and the same tolerance applies to tread depth. Inconsistent riser heights are the single most common failed-inspection item on stairs because an uneven riser is a trip hazard. Build every riser and tread to the same dimension and verify the maximum riser and minimum tread against IRC R311.7.5 for your adopted edition.

dimensions.note: IRC R311.7.5.2, measured nosing to nosing
required where treads are less than 11 in; IRC R311.7.5.3

steps: Measure total rise floor to floor and divide into equal risers at or below 7 3/4 in.
Set a uniform tread depth of at least 10 in.
Lay out the stringer so every riser and every tread is identical.
Check the finished flight: no riser or tread varies more than 3/8 in from another.
common_mistakes: Inconsistent riser heights over 3/8 in variance, usually at the top or bottom step.
Forgetting the finished floor thickness, which changes the first and last riser.
Treads shallower than 10 in without the required nosing.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: FBC Residential 8th Edition follows IRC R311.7 stair geometry.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: NC RC 2018 (2015 IRC) uses the same 7 3/4 in riser and 10 in tread limits; confirm section numbering.

title: Stair headroom and width
text: A residential stair needs a minimum clear width and a minimum headroom measured vertically from the tread nosing line to any ceiling or obstruction above. Headroom is easy to pinch at a landing or where a stair passes under a floor above. Provide at least 6 feet 8 inches of headroom and the required clear width, and verify against IRC R311.7.1 and R311.7.2 for your adopted edition.

dimensions.note: IRC R311.7.2, measured vertically from the nosing line
IRC R311.7.1, above the handrail height

steps: Draw the stair in section and project the nosing line to the ceiling above.
Confirm at least 6 ft 8 in clear at every point, including under the floor above and at landings.
Confirm 36 in clear width, allowing for the handrail projection.
common_mistakes: Headroom pinch where the stair passes under the floor framing above.
Measuring width below the handrail and losing clear width.
Landing built too small, reducing headroom at the turn.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: Same as IRC R311.7 under the FBC Residential 8th Edition.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: Same 6 ft 8 in headroom under the enforced NC RC 2018.

title: Handrails and guards on stairs
text: A handrail is required on at least one side of a stair with four or more risers, set at a consistent height above the nosing line with a grippable profile. A guard is required along open sides where the walking surface is more than 30 inches above the floor or grade below, and the guard must resist a 4 inch sphere passing through so a child cannot slip through. Set the handrail height in the required range, choose a grippable cross section, and provide guards with proper infill, verifying against IRC R311.7.8 and R312 for your adopted edition.

dimensions.note: IRC R311.7.8.1, above the nosing line
IRC R312.1.2, where over 30 in drop
IRC R312.1.3; stair triangle allows 6 in

steps: Install a continuous graspable handrail on stairs of 4 or more risers at 34 in to 38 in.
Add guards at open sides over 30 in above the surface below at 36 in minimum height.
Space balusters so a 4 in sphere cannot pass (6 in at the triangular stair opening).
Confirm the handrail profile is grippable per the code cross-section rule.
common_mistakes: Guard shorter than 36 in or balusters wider than the 4 in sphere rule.
A wide flat 2x6 cap used as a handrail, which is not a grippable profile.
No handrail on a short but 4-riser run.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: FBC Residential 8th Edition follows IRC R311.7.8 and R312.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: NC RC 2018 uses the same handrail range and 4 in sphere guard rule.
